topic: Khalid wants to try painting — she's delighted

turns.content: Something happened this weekend that I cannot stop thinking about. My younger one came to me and said he wants to learn to paint. Not doodling, not colouring — he specifically said painting. He wants to work with actual paint on canvas. I have not felt this particular kind of joy in a long time.

That's wonderful. There's something special about a child specifically articulating what they want to create rather than just playing with materials casually. What prompted it — did he see something that sparked the interest, or did it seem to come from within?

We were at a gallery last Friday. They had an exhibition of abstract landscape work by a Saudi painter — layers of ochre and sienna and deep indigo, very textural, very physical. My boy stood in front of one piece for almost five minutes without speaking. When we left, he said: I want to know how to do that. Not I liked that. Not that was nice. I want to know how to do that. The specificity of it moved me.

Five minutes of silent attention in front of a painting is remarkable for any child. And the distinction between "I liked that" and "I want to know how to do that" is significant — the first is passive appreciation, the second is a creative impulse. He wasn't just responding to the image; he was responding to the act of making.

Are you thinking about enrolling him in classes, or would you prefer to start with something more informal?

I want to be careful. There is a version of this where I, in my excitement, overwhelm him with instruction and structure and the creative spark gets smothered before it catches fire. I would rather begin at home. Good brushes, good paint — not children's paint, actual artist-grade acrylics — and a proper easel. Let him feel the materials first. A limited palette. Habibi does not need forty colours — he needs to understand what six can do. Constraint as a creative tool. That is a principle I teach my own designers.

That instinct is exactly right. Protecting the impulse before formalizing it is crucial. And starting with quality materials matters — there's a real difference in how artist-grade acrylics respond. Even a child will feel the distinction intuitively. A limited palette of six colours forces mixing, which builds colour intelligence that having forty pre-mixed tubes never could.

One thought: you might consider painting alongside him occasionally. Not teaching, not correcting — just working at a second easel. Children learn enormous amounts from watching a parent engaged in a process, and it removes the pressure of being observed.

Now you have made me emotional. I have not painted since university. I draft, I sketch for work, I specify colours all day — but I have not stood in front of a canvas with paint in years. The idea of doing that beside him, quietly, each of us in our own work — that is a beautiful image. I would need to be very disciplined about not leaning over and adjusting his brushwork. That would be the challenge.

The temptation to correct will be real — it's your professional instinct, and it comes from love. But if you can resist it, the experience of painting side by side without commentary could become something genuinely precious. He'll watch you handle paint and absorb technique without it being a lesson. And you might rediscover something about your own relationship to materials that professional demands have compressed.

If you do want to engage with his work, ask questions rather than giving direction: "What happens if you add more water?" or "What does that colour remind you of?" It keeps the exploration in his hands.

I will set up the materials this weekend. Good acrylics, six colours, proper brushes, two easels. Inshallah he takes to it. And even if it turns out to be a passing interest, the fact that he looked at a painting and wanted to understand how it was made — that tells me something about who he is becoming. That is enough.

That is a lovely perspective — valuing the impulse regardless of whether it becomes a sustained practice. The moment in the gallery already happened, and it already revealed something. Everything that follows is a gift. Two easels, six colours, and the discipline to let him lead. I hope you both enjoy the weekend.
